retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia for Labor, Delivery, and Cesarean Section in High-Risk Heart Disease -- Issues of Concern -- Conversion of Epidural Analgesia to Surgical Anesthesia
retrieved_docs.content: Epidural analgesia with a combined low-dose local anesthetic and an opioid infusion is generally inadequate to provide surgical anesthesia for a Cesarean section. When a vaginal delivery is converted to a Cesarean section, a dense block with either 15 to 20mL of 2% lidocaine with 1 to 200,000 epinephrine or 15 to 20 mL of 3% 2-chloroprocaine is preferred. Chloroprocaine has the advantage of slightly faster onset and metabolism by plasma esterases, so accidental intrathecal injection is inconsequential. Successful conversion to segmental anesthesia is essential in patients with cardiac disease who may not be able to tolerate general anesthesia. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Steroid Injections -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Absolute contraindications to epidural steroid injections include: Systemic infection or local infection at the site of injection Bleeding diathesis or full anticoagulation Significant allergic reaction or hypersensitivity to contrast, anesthetic, or corticosteroid Local malignancy Patient refusal Relative contraindications to epidural steroid injections: Uncontrolled diabetes mellitus Congestive heart failure Pregnancy due to fluoroscopy

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia for Labor, Delivery, and Cesarean Section in High-Risk Heart Disease -- Issues of Concern -- Neuraxial Anesthesia
retrieved_docs.content: The 2 main methods of neuraxial anesthesia during labor and delivery are epidural anesthesia and combined spinal-epidural anesthesia. Epidural anesthesia generally has a slower onset to peak effectiveness at 15 to 20 minutes and provides greater sacral dermatomal coverage with more uniformity in the sensory blockade. Combined spinal-epidural is preferred for initiating analgesia in cases of advanced labor due to its expedited onset in 2 to 5 minutes. Some practices are performing dural puncture epidural anesthesia in place of combined spinal-epidural. [8] [9] Modern labor analgesia regimens for epidurals typically consist of a combination of a dilute local anesthetic with a low-dose opioid (ie, bupivacaine and fentanyl). The combination of 2 low-dose analgesics reduces the risk of local anesthetic systemic toxicity and a high or total spinal anesthetic (encountered with unintentional intrathecal injection). Hemodynamic effects and placental transfer are minimized via this technique. Dilute local anesthetics help mitigate the risk of motor block, which would necessitate instrumental delivery due to compromised ability to push. Initiation of epidural anesthesia involves a bolus of an amide local anesthetic (ie, bupivacaine or ropivacaine) with a lipid-soluble opioid (ie, fentanyl or hydromorphone). After an initial bolus, maintenance of epidural anesthesia can be obtained through various combinations of techniques, including continuous infusion, intermittent bolus, or patient-controlled bolus. An opioid-only intrathecal dose, such as 25 micrograms of fentanyl, provides excellent analgesia for the first and second stages of labor while maintaining hemodynamic stability. Still, it is associated with a high incidence of pruritis. [10] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia for Labor, Delivery, and Cesarean Section in High-Risk Heart Disease -- Clinical Significance -- Coronary Artery Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with coronary artery disease should be maintained at a normal heart rate via excellent analgesia control and avoidance of beta-agonists that may precipitate an increase in heart rate. Epidural anesthesia is the mainstay of pain control in this population. A local anesthetic bolus through an epidural can significantly decrease systemic vascular resistance, potentially precipitating tachycardia; thus, slow titration is preferred. Beta-blockers should be continued throughout the labor and delivery process. Early institution of epidural anesthesia with a maintenance infusion reduces both preload and afterload, which subsequently reduces myocardial oxygen demand in these patients sensitive to ischemia. Intraarterial pressure monitoring should be considered, and phenylephrine should be the vasopressor of choice, given the reduction of systemic vascular resistance during pregnancy. Cardiac delivery is preferred for vaginal deliveries as the force of uterine contraction is the main factor causing fetal descent as opposed to a Valsalva maneuver. Valsalva maneuvers may result in intolerable hemodynamic changes in high-risk patients. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia for Labor, Delivery, and Cesarean Section in High-Risk Heart Disease -- Clinical Significance -- Mitral Stenosis
retrieved_docs.content: A key axiom in management includes excellent labor analgesia to prevent tachycardic responses to pain. This is best accomplished with a segmental lumbar epidural. Neuraxial anesthesia minimizes catecholamine-induced increases in heart rate. If a cardiac delivery is attempted, epidural anesthesia is ideal, allowing for both pain control and fetal descent without significant hemodynamic changes. If an epidural is unable to be obtained, a low spinal with opiates is the next best option, as it can help curb unfavorable hemodynamic responses. When neuraxial anesthesia is contraindicated, a pudendal nerve block or systemic analgesia with opiates can be considered a last resort. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural (Archived) -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Epidural analgesia is indicated for pain management during or after surgical procedures involving the regions previously mentioned, severe chronic pain in the context of malignancy, and for labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia for Labor, Delivery, and Cesarean Section in High-Risk Heart Disease -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanical Prosthetic Valve
retrieved_docs.content: Risks of anticoagulation with an anesthetic technique should be carefully weighed. Anticoagulation significantly increases the risk of epidural hematoma when using neuraxial anesthesia. Regional anesthetic techniques should not be attempted unless anticoagulation has been discontinued for at least 12 to 24 hours. One method for patients at significant risk of thrombosis is to continue heparinization throughout labor and delivery while using systemic analgesia with an opiate such as fentanyl for labor and then switching to general anesthesia for delivery. General anesthesia should be used for Cesarean section patients who are therapeutically anticoagulated. Anticoagulation significantly increases the risk of peripartum and postpartum bleeding, so the potential for hemorrhage should be anticipated, and blood products should be made readily available. Postpartum patients should be evaluated for evidence of valvular clotting and obstetric bleeding. [2] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia for Labor, Delivery, and Cesarean Section in High-Risk Heart Disease -- Clinical Significance -- Mitral Stenosis
retrieved_docs.content: Cesarean section should also be approached similarly to vaginal delivery. Epidural anesthesia is preferred as the primary means of anesthesia. The prudent anesthesiologist must be mindful of the added dangers of operative delivery, such as fluid shifts secondary to anesthesia, operative blood loss, and fluid mobilization associated with the post-partum period. Ephedrine should be avoided as it may produce tachycardia. Low-dose phenylephrine is the vasopressor of choice. When general anesthesia is necessary, the biggest concerns involve direct laryngoscopy and extubation, as both of these events are associated with potential tachycardia. Due to its hemodynamic stability, general anesthesia is best maintained with generous opiates, adequate neuromuscular blockade, and potentially nitrous oxide.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia for Labor, Delivery, and Cesarean Section in High-Risk Heart Disease -- Issues of Concern -- Systemic Opioids
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic opioids are an excellent choice for patients who cannot obtain neuraxial anesthesia due to various contraindications, including therapeutic anticoagulation and difficult spinal anatomy. A common approach includes patient-controlled analgesia with 25 micrograms of intravenous fentanyl administered every 10 to 15 minutes with a maximum of 100 micrograms per hour. Remifentanil for patient-controlled analgesia has gained popularity due to its short half-life, plasma-esterase metabolism, and very low risk of significant transfer across the placenta. However, maternal sedation and risk of respiratory depression are higher for patients receiving remifentanil versus fentanyl alone for patient-controlled analgesia. The downside of administering opioids is the side effect of pruritis. Pruritis can be managed with low doses of naloxone without comprising pain control. Interestingly, pain control quality with systemic opioids has been shown in some studies to be similar to pain control with nitrous oxide alone, albeit at the cost of the environment and for a much shorter duration of action. [8] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia for Labor, Delivery, and Cesarean Section in High-Risk Heart Disease -- Clinical Significance -- Coronary Artery Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Ideally, if a Cesarean section is performed, the epidural should have sufficient time to be titrated slowly to a surgical level of blockade with hypotension managed with judicious fluid administration. Spinal anesthesia is less desirable due to immediate sympathetic blockade, which could trigger sudden hypotension and reflexive tachycardia. If general anesthesia is warranted, the anesthesiologist should be aware of the stimulus of tracheal intubation and attempt to maintain normal hemodynamic parameters during laryngoscopy and tracheal intubation. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia for Labor, Delivery, and Cesarean Section in High-Risk Heart Disease -- Other Issues -- Delivery Planning
retrieved_docs.content: Cesarean section is preferred in all patients suffering from Ehlers-Danlos Type IV, Marfan, or Loeys-Dietz syndrome with a measured aortic diameter >4.5 cm and patients with concurrent bicuspid aortic valve and an aortic diameter >5.0 cm. Mothers with intractable heart failure typically do not tolerate labor well and should be evaluated for cesarean section. Mothers who are on therapeutic anticoagulation and are unable to receive neuraxial anesthesia should receive general anesthesia for Cesarean sections. Otherwise, a properly placed epidural catheter or combined spinal-epidural approach is recommended in most cases. Another option is a "cardiac vaginal delivery."  Utilizing this method involves waiting until complete cervical dilation is attained, then allowing the uterus to "labor down" the fetus instead of instructing the mother to push. Forceps or a vacuum assist device can complete the delivery once the head is low enough. Avoidance of the Valsalva maneuver is thought to be hemodynamically favorable in patients with cardiac conditions to avoid preload reductions and afterload increases.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia for Labor, Delivery, and Cesarean Section in High-Risk Heart Disease -- Clinical Significance -- Pulmonary Hypertension
retrieved_docs.content: The recommended mode of delivery is vaginal delivery with epidural anesthesia. Epidural anesthesia reduces pain, which, therefore, reduces oxygen consumption and hemodynamic variability. An emphasis should be placed on minimizing increases in pulmonary vasculature resistance. Supplemental oxygen should be utilized throughout labor and delivery to prevent hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction. This patient population could also benefit from intrathecal opiates due to their hemodynamic stability and enhanced ability to reduce pain. Oxytocin should be used with caution as it can decrease systemic vascular resistance while also elevating pulmonary vascular resistance; this results in decreased cardiac output due to decreased left ventricular filling. Cesarean section in this patient population is associated with increased maternal mortality. Although a dense neuraxial blockade may seem ideal, it may reduce right ventricular afterload too much, resulting in right ventricular failure. General anesthesia with an opioid-based technique may allow better control over pulmonary pressures, especially in accompaniment with a pulmonary artery catheter. Nitrous oxide is ill-advised since it increases pulmonary vascular resistance. Post-partum, the patient should be monitored for signs of right-sided heart failure. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Patient-Controlled Analgesia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Patient-controlled analgesia is used to treat acute, chronic, postoperative, and labor pain. A variety of medications can be used for patient-controlled analgesia, which is administered intravenously (IV), through an epidural or peripheral nerve catheter, or transdermally. This activity outlines and reviews the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients who undergo patient-controlled analgesia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium is classified under the broader category Indications for care in pregnancy; labor; and delivery, indicating that it is a subset of the clinical indications guiding obstetric management. Within this group of obstetric complications, diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium is recognized as a specific type of complication, highlighting that gestational diabetes and related glucose intolerance are important conditions to consider when evaluating and treating patients during pregnancy, labor, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2038
retrieved_docs.content: Previous cesarean section complicating pregnancy or childbirth is recognized as a specific manifestation of a previous c-section, indicating that a prior cesarean delivery can create complications in subsequent pregnancy or childbirth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Central nervous system complications of anesthesia or other sedation in labor and delivery, when delivered and noted with a postpartum complication, are regarded as other and unspecified complications of birth that affect the management of the mother during the puerperium. This group of complications is further classified under the broader category of “التأثير المتأخر لمضاعفات الحمل والولادة والنفاس,” which denotes the delayed effects of pregnancy, childbirth, and postpartum complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2081
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac complications that arise from anesthesia or other sedation administered during labor and delivery, especially when accompanied by a postpartum complication, are categorized as “Other and unspecified complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother.” This broader category is further classified under the concept of “التأثير المتأخر لمضاعفات الحمل والولادة والنفاس,” which refers to the delayed effects of pregnancy, childbirth, and postpartum complications. Consequently, such cardiac events are understood as part of the spectrum of maternal complications that can influence postpartum care and require specific attention in the management of the mother after delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2372
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance that arises during pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is recognized as a specific type of complication within the broader category of complications of pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium. This condition represents a distinct obstetric complication where altered glucose metabolism adversely affects maternal and fetal health, underscoring the importance of monitoring and managing glucose levels throughout gestation, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2306
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is a subcategory within the broader group of Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium. Among the many obstetric complications encompassed by this larger category, diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium represents a specific type of complication. This relationship underscores that gestational diabetes and related disorders of glucose metabolism are recognized as important pregnancy‑related complications and are included in the overall spectrum of conditions that can affect maternal and fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period. Effective identification and management of these glucose‑related complications are essential components of comprehensive obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: Induction and delivery procedures encompass a range of interventions used to initiate and manage labor, with medical induction of labor identified as a specific type of procedure within this broader category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2040
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac complications of anesthesia or other sedation in labor and delivery, delivered, with mention of postpartum complication represent a more specific subset within the broader category of مضاعفات الحمل (pregnancy complications). Likewise, اضطراب جنيني (fetal disorder) is also classified under مضاعفات الحمل, indicating that fetal disorders are considered a type of pregnancy complication. Together, these relationships show that both anesthesia‑related cardiac events during labor and fetal disorders are encompassed by the overarching concept of pregnancy complications, underscoring the need for careful obstetric monitoring and management of these distinct yet related conditions.

retrieved_docs.content: in labor and delivery (with postpartum mention) and central nervous system complications of anesthesia or other sedation in labor and delivery (with postpartum mention). Both anesthesia‑related cardiac and CNS complications are classified under “Other and unspecified complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother,” highlighting their impact on maternal care after delivery, and they are identified as narrower subsets of pregnancy complications, emphasizing that they arise in the context of gestation and delivery. Fetal disorder (“اضطراب جنيني”) is recorded as a parent of pregnancy complications, underscoring the contribution of intrinsic fetal abnormalities to maternal obstetric risk. The late‑effect category “التأثير المتأخر لمضاعفات الحمل والولادة والنفاس” (late effect of pregnancy, birth, and postpartum complications) is classified as a type of pregnancy complication, linking long‑term sequelae to the acute obstetric event. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium encompass a wide range of obstetric conditions that affect the mother, fetus and labor course. Early or threatened labor (مخاض كاذب, “false labor”) is a clinical presentation that includes cervical shortening, which is recorded as a measurable obstetric finding (short cervical length) in the second trimester, third trimester or an unspecified trimester. Cervical shortening is therefore a subtype of early or threatened labor and is linked to cervical stenosis, both classified as obstetric cervical abnormalities. Maternal systemic disorders that complicate pregnancy include hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium (ضغط الدم) and its variant hypertension ± vomiting, as well as diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance. Hypertension is positioned as a circulatory‑system disease that can co‑occur with vomiting and influences both maternal and fetal outcomes. Pregnancy‑related anatomic and clinical findings such

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2843
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical induction of labor is classified as a specific type of induction and delivery procedure, meaning it falls under the broader category of interventions used to initiate and manage childbirth. Recognizing surgical induction of labor as a subset of induction and delivery procedures helps clinicians organize treatment options, select appropriate methods for initiating labor, and coordinate patient care within the overall framework of obstetric management.

Recognizing these interrelated conditions enables clinicians to anticipate potential delivery challenges, plan appropriate interventions (such as external cephalic version, cervical assessment, or operative delivery), and optimize outcomes for both mother and child.
